charts.name: Liebherr 470 EC-B 20
charts.description: Flat-top tower crane with 83m max jib, 20 tonne max capacity. Features LM 1 and Load-Plus modes.

questions.question: According to the load chart (LM 1 mode, 83m jib), what is the maximum capacity at the jib tip (83.0m radius)?

options.explanation: Correct. At 83.0m radius in LM 1 mode, the capacity is 1.80 tonnes (1,800 kg). This is shown in the first row of the LM 1 table.
Incorrect. 2,200 kg is the tip capacity in Load-Plus mode, not standard LM 1 mode.
Incorrect. 2,500 kg is the capacity at 80.0m radius, not at the jib tip.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at approximately 75-77m radius.

questions.question: What is the maximum lifting capacity of this crane (at minimum radius)?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The maximum capacity is higher than this.
Incorrect. 19,000 kg is available at 24.4m in LM 1 mode, but Load-Plus offers more.
Correct. The 470 EC-B 20 has a maximum capacity of 20 tonnes (20,000 kg). The '20' in the model name indicates this rating.
Incorrect. This exceeds the crane's maximum rating.

questions.question: Using the LM 1 chart (83m jib), a load weighs 8,000 kg including rigging. What is the maximum radius where this load can be safely lifted?

options.explanation: Incorrect. At 47.5m, capacity is 9.10t which would work, but you can reach further.
Correct. At 50.0m radius, the capacity is 8.50 tonnes (8,500 kg). At 52.5m, capacity drops to 7.90 tonnes (7,900 kg) which is less than the 8,000 kg load.
Incorrect. At 52.5m, capacity is only 7.90t (7,900 kg), which is less than the 8,000 kg load.
Incorrect. Capacity at 55m is only 7.30t (7,300 kg).

questions.question: According to the LM 1 chart (83m jib), what is the capacity at 40.0m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the capacity at 42.5m radius.
Correct. At 40.0m radius, the LM 1 chart shows 11.10 tonnes (11,100 kg) capacity.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 37.5m radius.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 35.0m radius.

questions.question: If the hook block weighs 1,000 kg and rigging weighs 500 kg, what is the maximum LOAD weight that can be lifted at 60.0m radius (LM 1 mode, 83m jib) where chart shows 6,100 kg capacity?

options.explanation: Incorrect. You must subtract hook block and rigging weight from capacity.
Incorrect. Check your subtraction.
Correct. 6,100 kg capacity - 1,000 kg block - 500 kg rigging = 4,600 kg available for the actual load.
Incorrect. This subtracts too much.

questions.question: What is the difference in tip capacity between LM 1 mode and Load-Plus mode at 83.0m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The difference is larger than 200 kg.
Correct. LM 1 shows 1,800 kg at 83m tip, while Load-Plus shows 2,200 kg. The difference is 400 kg (2,200 - 1,800 = 400).
Incorrect. Check the values in both tables.
Incorrect. The difference is not this large.

questions.question: Using Load-Plus mode (83m jib), what is the capacity at 50.0m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the LM 1 capacity at 50m, not Load-Plus.
Correct. The Load-Plus table shows 9.50 tonnes (9,500 kg) at 50.0m radius, compared to 8.50 tonnes in LM 1 mode.
Incorrect. Check the Load-Plus table more carefully.
Incorrect. This exceeds the Load-Plus capacity at 50m.

questions.question: A contractor needs to lift 12,000 kg at 35-40m radius range. Using LM 1 mode (83m jib), is this possible?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Check the capacities carefully at both ends of the range.
Correct. At 35m, capacity is 13,000 kg (sufficient). At 40m, capacity drops to 11,100 kg (insufficient for 12,000 kg load).

options.text: No, the crane cannot lift 12,000 kg at either radius
options.explanation: Incorrect. The crane can lift 12,000 kg at shorter radii.
Incorrect. The capacity is actually lower at 40m.

questions.question: According to the chart notes, the lifting capacities are valid up to what hoisting height?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The chart allows for greater hoisting heights.
Correct. The note states 'Lifting capacities valid up to 50 m hoisting height.' Beyond this, different capacities may apply.
Incorrect. While the crane can reach 80m height, the standard capacity values are valid to 50m.
Incorrect. This exceeds the stated limitation.

questions.question: Looking at the LM 1 table, at what radius does capacity first drop below 5,000 kg (83m jib)?

options.explanation: Incorrect. At 62.5m, capacity is still 5,600 kg.
Correct. At 65.0m radius, capacity is 5,100 kg. At 67.5m, it drops to 4,600 kg - the first value below 5,000 kg.
Incorrect. At 67.5m capacity is already below 5,000 kg (4,600 kg). The drop below 5,000 kg occurs between 65m and 67.5m.
Incorrect. Capacity is already well below 5,000 kg at this radius (4,100 kg).

questions.question: A precast concrete panel weighs 9,000 kg. Rigging and hook block total 1,200 kg. Using LM 1 mode (83m jib), what is the maximum radius for this lift?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The crane can reach further with this total load.
Incorrect. Check the capacity at 47.5m.
Correct. Total load = 9,000 + 1,200 = 10,200 kg. At 47.5m, capacity is 9,100 kg (insufficient). At 45.0m, capacity is 9,700 kg (insufficient). At 42.5m, capacity is 10,400 kg (sufficient).
Incorrect. At 50m, capacity is only 8,500 kg.

questions.question: What advantage does Load-Plus mode provide compared to LM 1 mode?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Load-Plus affects capacity, not speed.

options.text: Greater lifting capacity at the same radius
options.explanation: Correct. Load-Plus mode provides increased lifting capacity throughout the working range, especially noticeable at longer radii.
Incorrect. Jib length is the same in both modes.
Incorrect. Load-Plus typically requires the same or more counterweight.

charts.name: Liebherr 195 HC-LH 6/12
charts.description: Luffing jib tower crane with 55m max jib. 6 tonne (single fall) / 12 tonne (2-fall) capacity. Features level luffing and Load-Plus modes.

questions.question: What type of crane is the 195 HC-LH, and how does it differ from a flat-top crane?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This describes a saddle jib crane, not the HC-LH.

options.text: Luffing jib crane that changes radius by raising/lowering the boom
options.explanation: Correct. The HC-LH is a luffing jib crane. Unlike flat-top cranes where the trolley travels along a horizontal jib, luffing cranes change radius by changing the boom angle (luffing up or down).
Incorrect. This is a tower crane, not a mobile crane.
Incorrect. Hammerhead describes a different crane configuration.

questions.question: What is the maximum lifting capacity of the 195 HC-LH when using 2-fall reeving?

options.explanation: Incorrect. 6,000 kg is the maximum for single-fall reeving.
Incorrect. 8,000 kg is the 2-fall capacity with the 55m jib, not the absolute maximum.
Incorrect. 10,000 kg is the 2-fall capacity with the 50m jib.
Correct. With 2-fall reeving on the 40m or 45m jib, the crane can lift 12 tonnes (12,000 kg) at minimum radius. The '6/12' in the model name indicates these capacities.

questions.question: Using the LM 1 chart with 55m jib in 2-fall configuration, what is the capacity at 30.0m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the single-fall capacity at 30m.
Correct. At 30.0m radius with 55m jib in 2-fall mode, capacity is 3.89 tonnes (3,890 kg).
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 27.5m radius.
Incorrect. This is the 2-fall capacity at 30m with a 50m jib, not 55m.

questions.question: With the 50m jib in 2-fall configuration (LM 1), what is the maximum capacity at 15.0m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the single-fall maximum.
Incorrect. This is the 55m jib 2-fall capacity.
Correct. With the 50m jib in 2-fall mode, the maximum capacity is 10 tonnes (10,000 kg) at 15.0m radius.
Incorrect. 12,000 kg is only achievable with shorter jib configurations (40m or 45m).

questions.question: According to the chart notes, up to what hoisting height are the lifting capacities valid?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The 195 HC-LH allows higher hoisting heights.
Incorrect. Check the chart notes.
Correct. The chart states 'Lifting capacities valid up to 100 m hoisting height.' Above 100m, capacity is reduced by the additional rope weight.
Incorrect. The stated limit is 100m.

questions.question: What happens to lifting capacity when hoisting height exceeds 100m?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The chart notes specify a reduction.

options.text: Capacity is reduced by the additional rope weight
options.explanation: Correct. The chart notes state that over 100m hoisting height, lifting capacity is reduced by the additional rope weight.
Incorrect. The crane can operate higher, but with reduced capacity.
Incorrect. This is not what the chart states.

questions.question: Using the 45m jib with 2-fall reeving (LM 1), what is the capacity at 25.0m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the capacity at 27.5m.
Correct. At 25.0m radius with the 45m jib in 2-fall mode, capacity is 7.20 tonnes (7,200 kg).
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 22.5m radius.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 20.0m radius.

questions.question: A load weighs 5,500 kg including rigging. Using the 50m jib with 2-fall reeving (LM 1), what is the maximum radius for this lift?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Capacity at 25m is 6,810 kg - the crane can reach further.
Correct. At 27.5m, capacity is 6,070 kg (sufficient). At 30.0m, capacity drops to 5,420 kg which is less than the 5,500 kg load.
Incorrect. At 30m, capacity is only 5,420 kg which is insufficient.
Incorrect. Capacity at 32.5m is only 4,850 kg.

questions.question: What is the 'Level luffing' feature mentioned in the Liebherr Connect specifications?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Level luffing is about maintaining hook height, not capacity.

options.text: Automatic retention of hook height when changing radius
options.explanation: Correct. Level luffing automatically maintains the hook height (lowering depth) when the boom angle changes. This prevents the load from rising or falling as the operator changes radius.
Incorrect. The boom angle changes to change radius.
Incorrect. This is unrelated to level luffing.

questions.question: Why does a shorter jib (e.g., 40m) allow higher maximum capacity than a longer jib (e.g., 55m)?

options.text: Shorter jibs are made of stronger steel
options.explanation: Incorrect. The material is the same.

options.text: Shorter jibs create less overturning moment at the same load
options.explanation: Correct. With a shorter jib, the maximum working radius is closer to the tower. This reduces the moment arm (leverage) and allows higher loads while maintaining crane stability.

options.text: Shorter jibs have more parts of line
options.explanation: Incorrect. Parts of line are determined by reeving configuration, not jib length.

options.text: The crane moves faster with shorter jibs
options.explanation: Incorrect. Speed is not the reason for capacity differences.

questions.question: What is the maximum reach (tip radius) when using single-fall reeving with the 55m jib?

options.explanation: Correct. The LM 1 table shows single-fall operation with 55m jib ranges from 3.0m to 30.9m radius (shown as '3.0 – 30.9' in the chart). Beyond 30.9m, 2-fall reeving is required.
Incorrect. Single-fall doesn't extend that far with the 55m jib.
Incorrect. This radius requires 2-fall reeving.
Incorrect. The jib tip radius is 55m, but single-fall has limited reach.

questions.question: Comparing single-fall vs 2-fall at 25.0m radius with 55m jib (LM 1), what are the respective capacities?

options.explanation: Correct. At 25.0m radius, single-fall provides 4.08t (4,080 kg) while 2-fall provides 4.90t (4,900 kg). The 2-fall configuration provides approximately 20% more capacity at this radius.
Incorrect. These are maximum capacities at minimum radius, not at 25m.
Incorrect. These are the capacities at 30m radius, not 25m.
Incorrect. These are the capacities at 20m radius.

charts.name: Liebherr 550 EC-H 20 Litronic
charts.description: Heavy-duty hammerhead tower crane with 81.5m max jib, 20 tonne max capacity. Features LM 1/LM 2 modes and Speed2Lift technology.

questions.question: What is the maximum lifting capacity of the 550 EC-H 20?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The crane can lift more than this at minimum radius.
Correct. The 550 EC-H 20 has a maximum capacity of 20 tonnes (20,000 kg). The '20' in the model name indicates this rating.
Incorrect. This exceeds the crane's rated capacity.
Incorrect. The '550' refers to the crane model series, not capacity.

questions.question: Using the LM 1 chart (81.5m jib), what is the capacity at the jib tip (81.5m radius)?

options.explanation: Correct. At 81.5m radius in LM 1 mode, the capacity is 3.50 tonnes (3,500 kg).
Incorrect. 4,000 kg is the tip capacity in LM 2 mode, not LM 1.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 71.5m radius.
Incorrect. This is higher than the actual tip capacity.

options.explanation: Incorrect. The 550 EC-H allows higher hoisting heights.

Incorrect. This crane's standard capacities are valid to 80m, unlike some luffing cranes that go to 100m.

questions.question: Using LM 1 mode (81.5m jib), what is the capacity at 41.5m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the capacity at 47.0m radius.
Correct. At 41.5m radius with the 81.5m jib in LM 1 mode, capacity is 8.68 tonnes (8,680 kg).
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 37.0m radius.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 33.0m radius.

questions.question: What does the 'r' value in parentheses (e.g., 'r = 83.7' for 81.5m jib) indicate?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The 'r' refers to radius, not rope.

options.text: The actual maximum radius including trolley position
options.explanation: Correct. The 'r' value indicates the true maximum working radius when the trolley is at the jib tip. For the 81.5m jib, the actual maximum radius is 83.7m.
Incorrect. Reinforced is indicated by 'r' after the model number, not in this context.
Incorrect. This is unrelated to slewing speed.

questions.question: Comparing LM 1 and LM 2 modes at 81.5m radius (81.5m jib), what are the respective capacities?

options.explanation: Correct. LM 2 mode provides 4.00 tonnes at the tip compared to 3.50 tonnes in LM 1 mode - an increase of 500 kg (14% improvement).
Incorrect. These values are higher than actual.
Incorrect. These values are lower than actual.
Incorrect. LM 2 provides higher capacity than LM 1.

questions.question: A structural steel beam weighs 15,000 kg. Using LM 1 mode (81.5m jib), what is the maximum radius for this lift?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Capacity at 21m is 18,700 kg - the crane can reach further.
Correct. At 25.0m, capacity is 15,340 kg (sufficient). At 29.0m, capacity drops to 13,070 kg which is less than the 15,000 kg load.
Incorrect. At 29m, capacity is only 13,070 kg which is insufficient.
Incorrect. Capacity at 33m is only 11,320 kg.

questions.question: With the 61.5m jib (LM 1), what is the capacity at 41.5m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the capacity with the 81.5m jib at 41.5m.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 47.0m with the 61.5m jib.
Correct. With the shorter 61.5m jib, capacity at 41.5m is 10.23 tonnes (10,230 kg) - higher than with the 81.5m jib due to reduced jib weight and moment.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 37.0m with the 61.5m jib.

questions.question: What is the 'Speed2Lift' feature mentioned in the Liebherr Connect specifications?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Speed2Lift is about speed optimization, not capacity.

options.text: Pure 2-fall operation with maximum hoist speed
options.explanation: Correct. Speed2Lift provides pure 2-fall operation with maximum hoist speed, optimizing productivity for lighter loads that don't require 4-fall reeving.
Incorrect. Speed2Lift relates to hoisting, not slewing.

options.text: Automatic speed adjustment based on load
options.explanation: Incorrect. While some systems do this, Speed2Lift specifically refers to optimized 2-fall operation.

questions.question: With the 51.5m jib (LM 1), what is the maximum capacity at minimum radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the capacity at 25.0m radius.
Correct. At minimum radius with any jib configuration, the crane can achieve its maximum rated capacity of 20 tonnes.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 29.0m radius.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 33.0m radius.

questions.question: If rigging weighs 800 kg and hook block weighs 1,200 kg, what is the maximum actual load weight at 61.5m radius (81.5m jib, LM 1) where chart shows 5,270 kg?

options.explanation: Incorrect. You must subtract rigging and hook block weight from capacity.
Incorrect. This only subtracts 1,000 kg.
Correct. 5,270 kg capacity - 800 kg rigging - 1,200 kg hook block = 3,270 kg available for the actual load.
Incorrect. This subtracts too much.

questions.question: Why does the 41.5m jib configuration allow full 20 tonne capacity out to 35.6m radius, while the 81.5m jib only allows it to 19.8m?

options.text: The 41.5m jib has a larger hook block
options.explanation: Incorrect. Hook block size doesn't explain this difference.

options.text: Shorter jibs weigh less and create smaller overturning moments
options.explanation: Correct. The shorter jib weighs less, so more of the crane's capacity can be used for the load rather than supporting jib weight. Additionally, shorter reach means smaller moment arms.
Incorrect. The material is the same.
Incorrect. While true, this doesn't directly explain the capacity range difference.

charts.name: Potain MDT 189
charts.description: Top-slewing tower crane with 197 ft max jib, 8.8 US ton capacity. Uses imperial units (feet/US tons). Features Potain Plus mode.

questions.question: UNIT CONVERSION TRAP: The Potain MDT 189 has a maximum capacity of 8.8 US tons. What is this in metric tonnes?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is a common trap! US tons and metric tonnes are NOT the same. 1 US ton = 2,000 lbs, while 1 metric tonne = 2,205 lbs.
Correct. 8.8 US tons × 2,000 lbs/USt ÷ 2,205 lbs/t = 7.98 tonnes ≈ 8.0 metric tonnes. Always verify which unit system a chart uses!
Incorrect. This would be the result if you multiplied instead of converting properly.
Incorrect. 17,600 is the capacity in pounds, not tonnes.

questions.question: Using the 197 ft jib, what is the capacity at the jib tip (197 ft radius)?

options.explanation: Correct. At maximum radius of 197 ft, the capacity is 1.7 US tons (3,400 lbs).
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 187 ft radius.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 180 ft radius.
Incorrect. 8.8 USt is the maximum capacity at minimum radius, not at the tip.

questions.question: TRICK QUESTION: At 82 ft radius with a 197 ft jib, the chart shows 6.6 USt capacity. At the same 82 ft radius with a 131 ft jib, capacity is 7.7 USt. Why is capacity HIGHER with the shorter jib at the SAME radius?

options.text: The chart has a printing error
options.explanation: Incorrect. The chart is correct - this is fundamental crane physics.

options.text: The 131 ft jib weighs less, leaving more capacity for the load
options.explanation: Correct. Even at identical radii, the longer jib weighs more. That extra jib weight uses up some of the crane's moment capacity, leaving less for the actual load.

options.text: The 131 ft jib has more parts of line
options.explanation: Incorrect. Parts of line affect hoisting, not capacity at a given radius.

options.text: Wind loads are lower on shorter jibs
options.explanation: Incorrect. While true, this isn't why the chart shows different capacities - the chart already accounts for standard conditions.

questions.question: Using the 180 ft jib, what is the capacity at 98 ft radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the capacity at 98 ft with the 197 ft jib.
Correct. With the 180 ft jib at 98 ft radius, capacity is 5.6 US tons.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 98 ft with the 148 ft jib.
Incorrect. This is the Potain Plus capacity, not standard.

questions.question: COMPLEX CALCULATION: A load weighs 9,500 lbs including rigging. Using the 164 ft jib, what is the maximum radius for this lift? (Note: 1 USt = 2,000 lbs)

options.explanation: Incorrect. At 89 ft, capacity is 6.6 USt (13,200 lbs) - you can reach further.
Incorrect. At 98 ft, capacity is 5.8 USt (11,600 lbs) - still more than needed, you can reach further.
Correct. 9,500 lbs ÷ 2,000 = 4.75 USt required. At 115 ft, capacity is 4.9 USt (9,800 lbs) - sufficient. At 121 ft, capacity drops to 4.5 USt (9,000 lbs) which is LESS than the 9,500 lbs needed.
Incorrect. At 131 ft, capacity is only 4.1 USt (8,200 lbs) - insufficient for the 9,500 lb load.

questions.question: What does 'Potain Plus' mode provide?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Potain Plus affects capacity, not trolley speed.

options.text: Increased lifting capacity at the same radius
options.explanation: Correct. Potain Plus load curves provide slightly higher capacities. For example, at 197 ft tip, standard is 1.7 USt while Plus mode offers 1.85 USt.
Incorrect. Jib length remains the same.
Incorrect. Potain Plus is about capacity, not power.

questions.question: TRAP QUESTION: The MDT 189 chart shows capacities for jibs from 82 ft to 197 ft. A rigger assumes the 82 ft jib can lift 8.8 USt anywhere along its length. Is this correct?

options.text: Yes, shorter jibs maintain full capacity throughout
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is a dangerous assumption! Even short jibs have capacity curves.

options.text: No, capacity still decreases as radius increases even on short jibs
options.explanation: Correct. Looking at the 82 ft jib row, capacity is 8.8 USt at 56 ft but drops to 8.1 USt at 82 ft radius. Capacity ALWAYS decreases with radius - there are no exceptions.

options.text: Yes, the 82 ft jib is rated at 8.8 USt for all radii
options.explanation: Incorrect. Check the load chart carefully - even the shortest jib shows decreasing capacity.

options.text: It depends on the hook height
options.explanation: Incorrect. While hook height affects some configurations, this doesn't make the statement true.

questions.question: Using the 148 ft jib, at what radius does capacity first drop below 5.0 US tons?

options.explanation: Incorrect. At 105 ft, capacity is still 5.8 USt.
Incorrect. At 115 ft, capacity is 5.2 USt - still above 5.0.
Correct. At 121 ft radius with 148 ft jib, capacity drops to 4.9 USt - the first value below 5.0 USt.
Incorrect. At 131 ft, capacity is 4.4 USt - already well below 5.0.

questions.question: COMPLEX: A contractor needs to lift 4.0 USt loads at 140 ft radius. Which is the SHORTEST jib that can perform this lift?

options.explanation: Correct. The 148 ft jib at 138 ft shows 4.2 USt, and at 148 ft tip shows 3.9 USt. Interpolating to 140 ft gives approximately 4.1 USt - just enough for the 4.0 USt load. This is the shortest jib that reaches 140 ft with sufficient capacity.

Incorrect. The 164 ft jib would work (showing 3.9 USt at 138 ft and 3.5 USt at 148 ft, ~3.8 USt at 140 ft) but wait - that's less than 4.0 USt! So 164 ft jib actually doesn't work. The 148 ft jib is the answer.
Incorrect. The 180 ft jib at 138 ft shows only 3.5 USt - insufficient for 4.0 USt load.
Incorrect. The 197 ft jib at 138 ft shows only 3.2 USt - insufficient.

questions.question: The chart shows the 33 LVF hoist has 951 ft of rope. If operating at 200 ft hook height with 4-part line, approximately how much horizontal rope travel is available?

options.explanation: Incorrect. With 4-part line, you use 4x the vertical distance in rope.
Incorrect. Check your calculation.

options.text: About 150 ft of effective travel after accounting for rope on drum
options.explanation: Correct. With 4-part reeving at 200 ft height, you use 800 ft of rope just for height (200 × 4). This leaves only 151 ft of rope, but some must remain on the drum. Actual travel is very limited at this height.
Incorrect. You cannot use all rope for travel - height uses rope too.

questions.question: TRICK: Looking at the base ballast table, why do taller tower configurations require MORE ballast even with the same jib length?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Tower weight is supported by the foundation, not ballast.

options.text: Wind loads increase with height, requiring more stability
options.explanation: Correct. Higher towers catch more wind and have a longer moment arm to the base. The ballast counteracts these increased overturning forces.

options.text: The chart has errors at different heights
options.explanation: Incorrect. The chart is correct - this is proper engineering.
Incorrect. Tower height doesn't increase lifting capacity.

questions.question: According to the specifications, what wind standard does this chart reference for out-of-service conditions?

options.explanation: Incorrect. While EN 14439 is mentioned, there's a specific US standard referenced.
Correct. The chart notes reference ASCE 7-10, Wind Zone with 115 mph ultimate design wind speed, reduced to 98 mph design speed using the 0.85 factor for temporary structures.
Incorrect. OSHA is a safety regulation, not a wind load standard.
Incorrect. This is a Canadian standard, not the one referenced.

questions.question: HARD CALCULATION: A 7,000 lb steel beam needs to be lifted at 120 ft radius. The hook block weighs 617 lbs and rigging weighs 400 lbs. Using the 164 ft jib, is this lift possible?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The margin is not substantial - calculate precisely.

options.text: Yes, but with only about 10% margin
options.explanation: Correct. Total load = 7,000 + 617 + 400 = 8,017 lbs = 4.01 USt. At 121 ft radius (closest chart point), capacity is 4.5 USt (9,000 lbs). Margin = (9,000 - 8,017) ÷ 9,000 = 10.9%. Lift is possible but margins are tight.

options.text: No, total load exceeds capacity at 120 ft
options.explanation: Incorrect. 8,017 lbs (4.01 USt) is less than the 9,000 lbs (4.5 USt) capacity at 121 ft.

options.text: Cannot determine without knowing hook height
options.explanation: Incorrect. The standard chart applies to normal operating conditions.

questions.question: The counter-jib ballast table shows CAU blocks at 7,937 lbs and CAV blocks at 2,425 lbs. For a 197 ft jib, the chart shows 3 CAU + 4 CAV blocks needed. What is the total counter-jib ballast weight?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Check your multiplication.
Correct. (3 × 7,937) + (4 × 2,425) = 23,811 + 9,700 = 33,511 lbs. The chart confirms '33,510 lbs' for this configuration.
Incorrect. This calculation is wrong.
Incorrect. This only accounts for some of the blocks.

questions.question: CRITICAL THINKING: An operator notices the Potain MDT 189 chart shows capacities in US tons while they're used to metric tonnes. They're planning a 7 metric tonne lift at 100 ft radius with the 164 ft jib. The chart shows 5.8 USt at 98 ft. Should they proceed?

options.text: Yes, 5.8 USt is greater than 7 tonnes
options.explanation: Incorrect. DANGER! You're comparing different units. 5.8 US tons ≠ 5.8 metric tonnes.

options.text: No, must convert units first - 7 tonnes = 7.7 USt which exceeds 5.8 USt capacity
options.explanation: Correct. 7 metric tonnes × 2,205 lbs/t ÷ 2,000 lbs/USt = 7.72 US tons required. Capacity of 5.8 USt is insufficient. This lift would overload the crane by 33%!

options.text: Yes, the difference between US tons and metric tonnes is negligible
options.explanation: Incorrect. The ~10% difference between unit systems is NOT negligible and can cause serious overloading.

options.text: Need more information about wind conditions
options.explanation: Incorrect. The unit conversion issue is the critical problem here.

charts.name: Potain MD 1600
charts.description: Heavy-duty top-slewing tower crane with 80m max jib. Dual capacity: 40 tonne (2-fall) or 64 tonne (4-fall). FEM 1.001-A3 standard.

questions.question: The MD 1600 has two capacity configurations: 40t and 64t. What determines which configuration is used?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Jib length affects radius, not the base capacity configuration.

options.text: The number of parts of line (reeving) - 2-fall for 40t, 4-fall for 64t
options.explanation: Correct. The 40t configuration uses 2-fall reeving (2 parts of line), while the 64t configuration uses 4-fall reeving (4 parts of line). More parts of line = higher capacity but slower hoisting.
Incorrect. Tower height affects stability, not the base lifting configuration.
Incorrect. The reeving must be physically configured - it's not just a setting.

questions.question: Using the 64t configuration (4-fall) with 80m jib, what is the capacity at 50m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the 40t configuration capacity at 50m.
Correct. In 64t mode with 80m jib, the capacity at 50m radius is 31 tonnes.
Incorrect. 32t is the capacity in the 'constant zone' between approximately 40-48m.
Incorrect. This is the maximum capacity of the 40t configuration.

questions.question: TRAP QUESTION: Looking at the 64t load curve for 80m jib, capacity stays constant at 32 tonnes between approximately 40m and 48.6m radius. Why does capacity plateau instead of continuously decreasing?

options.text: The chart has a printing error in this zone
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is intentional design, not an error.

options.text: The trolley motor limits load in this zone
options.explanation: Incorrect. Motor limits affect speed, not capacity directly.

options.text: The hoist winch capacity (32t) becomes the limiting factor, not the crane's moment capacity
options.explanation: Correct. In this zone, the crane's structural moment capacity would allow more than 32t, but the 4-fall reeving on the hoist drum is limited to 32 tonnes. This creates a 'flat' section on the load curve.

options.text: Wind loads are calculated differently in this radius range
options.explanation: Incorrect. Wind calculations don't create flat zones in load curves.

questions.question: The load curve shows deductions of '-5.8t' for the 64t configuration and '-3.5t' for the 40t configuration. What do these deductions represent?

options.text: Safety margin that must be maintained
options.explanation: Incorrect. These are actual weights, not safety factors.

options.text: Hook block and trolley weight that must be subtracted from chart capacity
options.explanation: Correct. The heavy-duty hook blocks for the 64t configuration weigh 5.8 tonnes. This must be deducted from chart capacities to determine actual load capacity.
Incorrect. Counterweight is separate from these deductions.
Incorrect. Wind loads are already factored into the base chart.

questions.question: COMPLEX: Using the 64t configuration (80m jib), a precast panel weighs 25 tonnes. The hook block weighs 5.8t and rigging weighs 2t. At what maximum radius can this lift be performed?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Total load is 32.8t. At 50m, capacity is 31t - insufficient.
Incorrect. Even in the constant zone, capacity is only 32t which is less than 32.8t total.
Correct. Total = 25 + 5.8 + 2 = 32.8 tonnes. At 40m, capacity is 34.3t (sufficient). At 42m (constant zone start), capacity drops to 32t which is insufficient.
Incorrect. While safe, this isn't the maximum radius - you can reach further.

questions.question: Using the 40t configuration (2-fall) with 80m jib, what is the capacity at 60m radius?

options.explanation: Correct. In 40t mode with 80m jib at 60m radius, capacity is 21 tonnes.
Incorrect. This is the 64t configuration capacity at 60m.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 50m, not 60m.
Incorrect. 20t is the capacity at 65m in the constant zone.

questions.question: TRICK: A rigger sees '40t' on the crane spec and assumes any load under 40 tonnes can be lifted anywhere. Looking at the 40t configuration with 80m jib, what is the ACTUAL capacity at 80m radius (jib tip)?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This dangerous assumption ignores how load charts work.
Correct. At the 80m tip, capacity is only 19.2 tonnes - less than half the maximum rating. And after subtracting hook block (3.5t), only ~15.7t is available for actual load.
Incorrect. 20t is the capacity in the constant zone (65-67m), not at the tip.
Incorrect. This is far higher than actual tip capacity.

questions.question: Why would a contractor choose the 40t (2-fall) configuration over the 64t (4-fall) configuration?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Both configurations are safe when used within their ratings.

options.text: Faster hoisting speeds - 2-fall hoists twice as fast as 4-fall for the same motor speed
options.explanation: Correct. With 2-fall reeving, the hook travels twice as fast as with 4-fall (for the same drum speed). For lighter loads that don't need 64t capacity, the 40t configuration provides much faster cycle times.
Incorrect. Counterweight requirements depend on jib configuration, not reeving.
Incorrect. Jib options are the same for both configurations.

questions.question: The chart references FEM 1.001-A3 standard. What does this indicate?

options.explanation: Incorrect. FEM is a European standard, not US.

options.text: European crane design and safety standard
options.explanation: Correct. FEM (Fédération Européenne de la Manutention) 1.001-A3 is the European standard for crane design, including load calculations, safety factors, and wind conditions.
Incorrect. CSA is Canadian, FEM is European.
Incorrect. FEM refers to a design standard.

questions.question: Using the 64t configuration with 50m jib, what is the capacity at maximum radius (50m)?

options.explanation: Correct. With the 50m jib in 64t mode, the tip capacity at 50m is 32 tonnes. This falls within the hoist-limited constant zone.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 40m with the 50m jib.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 35m.
Incorrect. 64t is only achievable at minimum radius.

questions.question: COMPLEX: Comparing the 64t (80m jib) to the 64t (50m jib) at 45m radius - which has higher capacity and why?

options.text: Same capacity - radius is what matters, not jib length
options.explanation: Incorrect. Jib length significantly affects capacity at the same radius.

options.text: 80m jib has higher capacity due to longer moment arm
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is backwards - longer jibs reduce capacity.

options.text: 50m jib has higher capacity (32t vs ~32t) - shorter jib means less jib weight consuming capacity
options.explanation: Correct. At 45m radius: 50m jib shows 32t capacity, while 80m jib shows approximately 32t (in constant zone). They're similar here because both hit the hoist limit, but generally shorter jibs allow higher capacity at same radius.
Incorrect. Both use 64t (4-fall) configuration.

questions.question: The mechanisms section shows the 270 LVF 100 hoist at 400V-50Hz can lift 20t at 51 m/min or 5t at 139 m/min. What principle does this demonstrate?

options.explanation: Incorrect. While true, this doesn't explain the speed relationship.

options.text: Inverse relationship between load and speed - power is constant, so lighter loads can be hoisted faster
options.explanation: Correct. The hoist motor has fixed power output. Power = Force × Speed. With constant power, reducing load (force) allows proportionally higher speed. This is why light loads fly up quickly.

options.text: The crane has variable speed drives
options.explanation: Incorrect. Variable drives exist but don't explain WHY speeds vary with load.

options.text: Safety limits require slower speeds for heavy loads
options.explanation: Incorrect. It's physics, not arbitrary safety limits.

questions.question: HARD: A 45-tonne transformer needs to be lifted at 30m radius. Which configuration and jib length combination would you select?

options.explanation: Incorrect. At 30m with 80m jib, capacity is only 40.7t - insufficient for 45t plus rigging.

Correct. At 30m with 50m jib, capacity is 50.4t. After deducting 5.8t for hook block, 44.6t remains. With minimal rigging (~1t), this is tight but workable. The 40m jib at 30m shows 53.6t - even better margin.
Incorrect. 40t configuration maximum is 40t - can't lift 45t regardless of radius.
Incorrect. While this would work (53.6t at 30m), it limits your radius more than necessary.

questions.question: What is the minimum radius where the 64t configuration (any jib) can lift its full 64 tonne capacity?

options.explanation: Correct. All jib configurations show 64t capacity at 6.5m minimum radius. This is where trolley is closest to the tower.
Incorrect. Capacity has already started decreasing by 10m.
Incorrect. At 24.1m with 80m jib, capacity has dropped to 61.4t.
Incorrect. Minimum working radius is 6.5m due to crane geometry.

questions.question: CRITICAL: An inexperienced operator reads '64t' on the crane and plans to lift a 60-tonne load at 25m radius using the 80m jib. The chart shows 61.4t at 24.1m and 49.4t at 25m. What critical error might they make?

options.text: No error - 60t is less than 64t maximum
options.explanation: Incorrect. This ignores how to read the load chart properly.

options.text: They might use the wrong row (different jib length)
options.explanation: Incorrect. While possible, this isn't the main trap.

options.text: At 25m radius, capacity is only 49.4t (not 61.4t) - they're misreading the interpolation AND forgetting the 5.8t hook block deduction
options.explanation: Correct. Multiple errors: 1) At exactly 25m, capacity is 49.4t, not the 61.4t shown at 24.1m. 2) Must deduct 5.8t for hook block, leaving only 43.6t for load. The 60t load would overload the crane by ~38%!
Incorrect. While true in extreme conditions, the chart trap is more immediate.

charts.name: Liebherr NC-LH 12-55
charts.description: Luffing jib tower crane with 55m max jib. 12 tonne max capacity (4-fall). Features Level Luffing, Load-Plus, and Micromove.

questions.question: What type of crane is the Liebherr NC-LH 12-55?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Flat-top cranes have horizontal jibs without a tower head.
Correct. The NC-LH is a luffing jib crane - the 'LH' stands for Luffing High. The jib angle can be changed to vary the radius while minimizing swing footprint.
Incorrect. Hammerhead cranes have a fixed horizontal jib with trolley travel.
Incorrect. Self-erecting cranes are smaller, mobile units.

questions.question: Using the 4-fall (12t) configuration with 45m jib, what is the maximum capacity at minimum radius (3.0m)?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the 2-fall capacity.
Incorrect. This is the 4-fall capacity with 55m jib at minimum radius.
Correct. With the 45m jib in 4-fall mode, the crane achieves its full 12 tonne rating at minimum radius.
Incorrect. This is the 4-fall capacity with 50m jib at minimum radius.

questions.question: TRAP: Looking at the 4-fall chart, the 55m jib shows 8t max capacity while the 45m jib shows 12t. Why can't the 55m jib achieve the full 12t rating?

options.text: The motor is weaker for longer jibs
options.explanation: Incorrect. The same hoist motor is used regardless of jib length.

options.text: The longer jib creates more overturning moment at the same load, so capacity must be reduced
options.explanation: Correct. Even at minimum radius (3.0m), the 55m jib's additional weight and moment arm reduce the maximum safe load. The crane's structural limits dictate lower starting capacity for longer jibs.

options.text: Longer jibs have slower hoisting speeds
options.explanation: Incorrect. Speed doesn't affect capacity ratings.

options.text: It's a printing error in the chart
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is intentional engineering design.

questions.question: The chart states 'Lifting capacities valid up to 100 m hoisting height.' What happens above 100m?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The crane can work at greater heights with the SNLK+ feature.

options.text: Capacity is reduced by the additional rope weight
options.explanation: Correct. Above 100m hoist height, the weight of the extra rope hanging down must be deducted from capacity. More rope = less available payload.
Incorrect. While wind affects operations, the 100m limit specifically relates to rope weight.

options.text: The crane must switch to 2-fall mode
options.explanation: Incorrect. Reeving configuration is independent of hoist height.

questions.question: What is the 'Level Luffing' feature mentioned in the NC-LH specifications?

options.text: Keeping the crane level on uneven ground
options.explanation: Incorrect. This describes foundation/base leveling, not level luffing.

options.text: Automatic retention of hook height when changing radius
options.explanation: Correct. Level luffing automatically adjusts hoist rope as the jib angle changes, keeping the hook at the same elevation. This allows horizontal load movement without vertical drift - critical for precise positioning.

options.text: Locking the jib at horizontal position
options.explanation: Incorrect. Luffing jibs are designed to operate at various angles.
Incorrect. This has nothing to do with level luffing.

questions.question: Using LM 1 mode (2-fall) with 55m jib, what is the capacity at 25m radius?

options.explanation: Correct. In LM 1 (2-fall) mode with 55m jib at 25m radius, capacity is 3.97 tonnes.
Incorrect. This is the 4-fall capacity at 25m.
Incorrect. 6t is only available at minimum radius in 2-fall mode.
Incorrect. This is a Load-Plus capacity, not standard LM 1.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'Load-Plus' mode on the NC-LH?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Load-Plus is for temporary, individual lifts only.

options.text: Temporary lifting capacity increase for individual lifts under controlled conditions
options.explanation: Correct. Load-Plus allows exceeding standard LM 1 ratings for specific lifts, with additional safety measures. Compare the Load-Plus chart to LM 1 - values are higher at each radius.
Incorrect. Reeving configuration is separate from Load-Plus.
Incorrect. Load-Plus doesn't change wind ratings.

questions.question: What is 'Micromove' used for on this crane?

options.text: Moving the crane on rails at slow speed
options.explanation: Incorrect. Micromove is an operational feature, not for crane travel.

options.text: Fine positioning mode for precise load placement
options.explanation: Correct. Micromove allows ultra-slow, precise movements for delicate positioning - essential when setting steel, glass panels, or other items requiring exact placement.
Incorrect. That's the ACS (Anti-Collision System) feature.
Incorrect. Remote control is a separate feature from Micromove.

questions.question: COMPLEX: Using 4-fall configuration with 40m jib, a load of 8 tonnes needs to be placed at 20m radius. Is this within capacity?

options.explanation: Correct. With 40m jib in 4-fall mode, capacity at 20m radius is 9.72t. The 8t load is safely within limits (82% capacity utilization).
Incorrect. Standard capacity is sufficient for this lift.
Incorrect. You're reading the 2-fall chart, not 4-fall.

options.text: Yes, but only with the 30m jib
options.explanation: Incorrect. The 40m jib has adequate capacity at this radius.

questions.question: Looking at the out-of-service positions, why does the 55m jib have a reduced out-of-service radius option (10.0m instead of 14.6m)?

options.text: To reduce wind loads when parked
options.explanation: Incorrect. While wind is a factor, this isn't the primary reason for the option.

options.text: To reduce the swing footprint in confined sites - achieved by raising jib to steeper angle (78.9° vs 74.0°)
options.explanation: Correct. The 'reduced' option raises the jib to 78.9° instead of 74.0°, bringing the jib tip closer to the tower and reducing the out-of-service swing radius from 14.6m to 10.0m. Essential for tight urban sites.
Incorrect. Out-of-service position doesn't affect fuel consumption.
Incorrect. It's an optional configuration, not a requirement.

questions.question: Why is a luffing jib crane like the NC-LH often preferred over a flat-top crane on urban construction sites?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Capacity depends on the specific crane model, not the type.

options.text: Smaller slewing radius - the jib can be raised to avoid oversailing neighboring properties
options.explanation: Correct. By raising the luffing jib to reduce radius, the crane avoids swinging over adjacent buildings or airspace. This is critical in dense urban areas where property boundaries and flight paths must be respected.
Incorrect. Speed depends on hoist motor, not crane type.
Incorrect. Luffing jibs are typically more expensive than flat-tops.

questions.question: The hoist specifications show the 45kW hoist can lift 6t at 0-39 m/min or 1.05t at 0-103 m/min. What determines the speed?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Physics constrains the relationship between load and speed.

options.text: The load weight - heavier loads mean slower hoisting due to motor power limits
options.explanation: Correct. With constant motor power (Power = Force × Speed), heavier loads require more force, leaving less power available for speed. Light loads can be hoisted much faster than heavy loads.
Incorrect. Jib length affects capacity, not hoist speed.
Incorrect. Weather doesn't directly determine hoist speeds.

questions.question: HARD: A contractor needs to lift 10 tonnes. Looking at the 4-fall chart, which jib length(s) can achieve this at minimum radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. While 30m works, it's not the only option.
Correct. All four shorter jibs achieve 12t at minimum radius. Only the 50m jib (10t) and 55m jib (8t) fall below the 10t requirement.

options.text: Any jib length can lift 10t at minimum radius
options.explanation: Incorrect. The 55m jib only achieves 8t at minimum radius.
Incorrect. While 50m achieves exactly 10t, shorter jibs exceed 10t.

questions.question: The counterweight table shows 5 blocks (28.0t total) for the 55m, 50m, 45m, 40m, and 35m jibs, but only 4 blocks (22.4t) for the 30m jib. Why?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Maximum capacity is the same (12t) for 30m-45m jibs.

options.text: The shorter jib creates less overturning moment, requiring less counterweight to balance
options.explanation: Correct. Counterweight must balance the jib's moment. A 30m jib at maximum load creates less moment than a 55m jib, so less counterweight is needed. Over-counterweighting would be wasteful and potentially unsafe.
Incorrect. Safety engineering, not cost, determines counterweight requirements.

options.text: The 30m configuration uses a different tower
options.explanation: Incorrect. Tower selection is independent of counterweight requirements.

questions.question: CRITICAL: An operator using 2-fall (LM 1) mode with 50m jib sees capacity of 6.00t at 17.5m radius. They then switch to 4-fall mode. What is the new capacity at the same radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. 4-fall mode provides higher capacity.
Incorrect. While 4-fall doubles the line pull, capacity doesn't simply double at all radii.
Incorrect. 9.95t is the capacity at 15m radius, not 17.5m.
Correct. In 4-fall mode with 50m jib at 17.5m radius, capacity is 8.71t. The increase isn't a simple doubling because the crane's structural moment limits also apply.

charts.name: WOLFF 355 B US
charts.description: Luffing jib tower crane with 60m max jib. 28 tonne max capacity (2-fall). ANSI B30.3-2012 compliant. Features WOLFF-Boost mode.

questions.question: What US safety standard does the WOLFF 355 B US comply with?

options.explanation: Incorrect. While OSHA regulations apply to crane operations, the crane design standard is different.
Correct. The chart states the crane is 'Compliant with ANSI Standard B30.3-2012 – Tower Cranes.' This is the American National Standard for tower crane safety.
Incorrect. FEM is a European standard, not US.
Incorrect. CSA is a Canadian standard.

questions.question: What is the WOLFF-Boost function and what restriction applies when using it?

options.explanation: Incorrect. WOLFF-Boost affects load capacity, not speed.

options.text: Allows exceeding load torque by up to 10%; hoisting and luffing can only move alternatingly (not simultaneously)
options.explanation: Correct. WOLFF-Boost permits up to 10% overload of the standard moment capacity, BUT hoisting gear and luffing gear must be moved one at a time - you cannot luff while hoisting.

options.text: Doubles the capacity; requires two operators
options.explanation: Incorrect. WOLFF-Boost only adds 10%, and operator count doesn't change.

options.text: Reduces wind limits by 10%; applies only in calm conditions
options.explanation: Incorrect. WOLFF-Boost doesn't relate to wind conditions.

questions.question: Using the 1-fall (14t max) configuration with 60m jib, what is the capacity at 45m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. 14t is only maintained up to about 25m radius with this jib.
Incorrect. This is the capacity with the 45m jib, not 60m jib.
Correct. With 60m jib in 1-fall mode at 45m radius, capacity is 7.4 tonnes (16,310 lb).
Incorrect. 4.4t is the tip capacity at 60m radius.

questions.question: Why does the 2-fall (28t) configuration show dashes (-) for the 55m and 60m jib lengths?

options.text: The chart has printing errors for longer jibs
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is intentional engineering limitation.

options.text: 2-fall reeving is not available for those jib lengths - the rope path is too long
options.explanation: Correct. With longer jibs, the total rope length required for 2-fall reeving exceeds the drum capacity, or the geometry doesn't allow it. The 2-fall charts only cover 30m-50m jib lengths.

options.text: Those jibs don't exist for this crane
options.explanation: Incorrect. 55m and 60m jibs exist - they just can't use 2-fall configuration.
Incorrect. It's a mechanical/geometric limitation, not a safety issue.

questions.question: Using 2-fall (28t) configuration with 40m jib, what is the maximum capacity at minimum radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. 28t is the theoretical max, but the chart shows actual capacity is lower.
Correct. With 40m jib in 2-fall mode at minimum radius (3.6m), capacity is 26.0 tonnes. The shorter jib doesn't generate enough moment to reach the full 28t capacity.
Incorrect. This is the 1-fall capacity.
Incorrect. This is the 1-fall capacity at 40m radius, not minimum radius.

questions.question: What happens to load capacity when the fly-jib is mounted on the WOLFF 355 B US?

options.text: Capacity increases because the fly-jib adds reach
options.explanation: Incorrect. Adding the fly-jib actually reduces main hook capacity.

options.text: Capacity decreases - the fly-jib's weight reduces available payload capacity
options.explanation: Correct. The chart shows bracketed values '(with mounted auxiliary hoist and fly-jib)' that are lower. For example, at 60m radius: 4.4t becomes 2.2t. The fly-jib's 1,260 kg weight must be deducted from capacity.

options.text: No change to main hook capacity
options.explanation: Incorrect. The fly-jib weight definitely affects main hook capacity.

options.text: Main hook is disabled when fly-jib is active
options.explanation: Incorrect. Both can be used, just with reduced main hook capacity.

questions.question: TRAP: Looking at the 1-fall chart with 60m jib, capacity at 60m radius is 4.4t. With fly-jib mounted, it drops to 2.2t. What does this tell you about the fly-jib assembly weight?

options.explanation: Correct. The difference (4.4t - 2.2t = 2.2t) represents the weight of the fly-jib assembly at that radius. This matches approximately with fly-jib (1,260 kg) plus auxiliary hoist and hook block components.
Incorrect. This would be the full capacity, not the fly-jib weight.
Incorrect. This would be the sum, not the difference.

options.text: Cannot determine fly-jib weight from this data
options.explanation: Incorrect. The capacity reduction directly tells you the weight impact.

questions.question: The WOLFF 355 B US has two hoist options: Hw 28110 FU (147 hp) and Hw 28132 FU (177 hp). What's the advantage of the more powerful hoist?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Both hoists have the same capacity ratings (14t/28t).

options.text: Faster hoisting speeds - 290 m/min vs 185 m/min for light loads
options.explanation: Correct. The Hw 28132 FU (177 hp) achieves 290 m/min (951 ft/min) for light loads vs 185 m/min (607 ft/min) for the Hw 28110 FU. Same capacity, faster cycle times.
Incorrect. Both have 920m hook path in single line mode.
Incorrect. Electric cranes don't use fuel, and the larger motor actually consumes more power.

questions.question: What is the maximum load moment of the WOLFF 355 B US?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This would be the model number misread as load moment.
Correct. The chart states 'Load moment: max. 5100 kNm'. This is the maximum rated moment (capacity × radius) the crane can handle.
Incorrect. This isn't the load moment.
Incorrect. This doesn't match any specification.

questions.question: COMPLEX: Using 2-fall configuration with 30m jib, capacity at 25m radius is 18.7t. Using 1-fall with the same 30m jib at 25m radius, capacity is 14.0t. Why isn't the 2-fall capacity double (28t)?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The values are correct engineering calculations.

options.text: 2-fall doubles the line pull but the crane's structural moment capacity limits the actual load at that radius
options.explanation: Correct. While 2-fall reeving doubles the hoist's lifting force, the crane's structural limit (5100 kNm moment) hasn't changed. At 25m × 28t = 700 kNm would be allowed, but 25m × 18.7t = 468 kNm. The full 28t is only achievable at shorter radii where moment is lower.

options.text: The motor can't provide enough power
options.explanation: Incorrect. Motor power affects speed, not maximum capacity.
Incorrect. Wind isn't the factor here.

questions.question: The fly-jib has what maximum capacity?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the main hoist 1-fall capacity.
Correct. The packing list shows 'Fly-Jib with hook block 8 t (17640 lb)'. The auxiliary hoist has a separate 8 tonne capacity.
Incorrect. This is the main hoist 2-fall capacity.
Incorrect. This is the reduced main hook capacity at tip with fly-jib mounted.

questions.question: Looking at the Operating Speeds table, the main hoist shows '0 - 14.0 t' at '0 ... 40 m/min' and '0 - 2.5 t' at '0 ... 185 m/min' for Hw 28110 FU. What principle does this demonstrate?

options.text: Heavier loads require slower speeds due to safety regulations
options.explanation: Incorrect. It's physics, not regulations.

options.text: Constant power output - heavier loads require more force, leaving less power available for speed
options.explanation: Correct. Power = Force × Speed. With the motor's fixed power output, lifting 14t (high force) limits speed to 40 m/min. Lifting only 2.5t (low force) allows speeds up to 185 m/min. The product (power) remains roughly constant.

options.text: The crane automatically limits speed based on load
options.explanation: Incorrect. While the crane may have speed controls, the relationship is fundamentally physics-based.

options.text: Rope wear is faster with heavy loads
options.explanation: Incorrect. This doesn't explain the speed-load relationship.

questions.question: HARD: A contractor needs to lift 15 tonnes to 35m radius. Looking at both 1-fall and 2-fall charts, what's the minimum jib length that can accomplish this?

options.explanation: Incorrect. 14.9t is less than 15t required, and this is at 30m radius, not 35m.

options.text: 35m jib in 2-fall mode (shows 17.5t at 25m and 14.9t at 30m)
options.explanation: Incorrect. At 35m radius, the 35m jib's 2-fall capacity isn't shown - it can't reach that radius in 2-fall mode.

options.text: Impossible - no configuration can lift 15t at 35m radius
options.explanation: Correct. Looking at all charts: 1-fall maxes at 14t everywhere. For 2-fall, the maximum useful radius for any jib is about 19.8m (30m jib). No combination achieves 15t at 35m radius. The 5100 kNm moment limit prevents it (15t × 35m = 525 kNm is within limit, but geometry restricts 2-fall to shorter radii).
Incorrect. The 40m jib's 2-fall mode only extends to about 18.6m radius.

questions.question: What is the slewing speed of the WOLFF 355 B US?

options.explanation: Correct. The Operating Speeds table shows slewing at 0.8 min⁻¹ (0.8 rpm). This means it takes about 75 seconds for a full 360° rotation.
Incorrect. This is the trolley/luffing speed, not slewing.
Incorrect. 1.8 min is the luffing time, not slewing speed.
Incorrect. This is the maximum hoisting speed for light loads.

questions.question: CRITICAL: The hook block weighs 540 kg (1,190 lb). Using 1-fall mode with 60m jib at 55m radius where capacity is 5.2t (11,510 lb), what's the maximum net load you can lift?

options.explanation: Incorrect. You must deduct the hook block weight from chart capacity.
Correct. Chart capacity 5.2t minus hook block 0.54t = 4.66t available for actual load. Always deduct hook block and rigging from chart capacity!
Incorrect. You subtract, not add the hook block weight.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 60m radius, not 55m.

charts.name: Liebherr 91 K
charts.description: Self-erecting tower crane with 48m max jib. 6 tonne max capacity. Features Sway Control, Vertical Line Finder, and self-erection system.

questions.question: What type of tower crane is the Liebherr 91 K?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Flat-top cranes have horizontal jibs and require separate assembly.
Incorrect. Luffing jibs change angle to vary radius, but aren't self-erecting.
Correct. The 91 K is a self-erecting crane - it folds for transport and can erect itself on site without a mobile crane. The 'K' designation indicates Liebherr's self-erecting series.
Incorrect. Hammerhead cranes are large stationary units requiring assembly.

questions.question: What European standard does the Liebherr 91 K comply with?

options.explanation: Incorrect. FEM is a different European standard used by some manufacturers.
Correct. The chart header states 'EN 14439:2009 – C25'. This is the European standard for tower crane safety.
Incorrect. ANSI is an American standard, not European.
Incorrect. This isn't the standard referenced on this chart.

questions.question: Using LM 1 mode with 48m jib at 30° position, what is the maximum capacity at 12m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is less than the actual capacity at minimum radius.
Correct. At 12m radius (minimum working radius), the 48m jib can lift its full 6,000 kg capacity.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 14m radius.
Incorrect. This is the tip capacity at 48m radius.

questions.question: Using LM 1 mode with 48m jib, what is the capacity at the jib tip (48m radius)?

options.explanation: Correct. At 48m radius with the 48m jib in LM 1 mode, capacity is 1,130 kg - less than 20% of the maximum rating.
Incorrect. This is the Load-Plus capacity at 48m, not standard LM 1.
Incorrect. Maximum capacity is only available at minimum radius.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 28m radius.

questions.question: What is Load-Plus mode and how does it affect capacity on the 91 K?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Load-Plus provides a moderate increase, not doubling.

options.text: Increased capacity for individual lifts with variant speeds and operating conditions
options.explanation: Correct. Load-Plus allows higher capacities than standard LM 1 (e.g., 1,380 kg vs 1,130 kg at 48m tip) but requires different operating speeds and conditions as noted in the chart.
Incorrect. That would be Load-Reduction, not Load-Plus.
Incorrect. Load-Plus works with the standard reeving configuration.

questions.question: What is the 'Sway Control' feature on the 91 K?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Slewing control is a separate function.

options.text: Load sway control assistant - reduces load pendulum/swing
options.explanation: Correct. Sway Control is an electronic system that counteracts load swing (pendulum effect) during crane movements. It helps position loads faster and more precisely by damping oscillations.
Incorrect. Wind monitoring is different from sway control.
Incorrect. Counterweight is mechanical, not an electronic control system.

questions.question: What is the 'Vertical Line Finder' feature?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Underground detection requires different equipment.

options.text: Side pull prevention - automatically positions hook directly over load to prevent diagonal rope pull
options.explanation: Correct. Vertical Line Finder prevents dangerous side-loading by automatically positioning the hook vertically above the load before lifting. Side pull causes rope damage and can destabilize loads.
Incorrect. Tower plumb is checked during erection, not during operation.

options.text: Aligns the jib with wind direction
options.explanation: Incorrect. This isn't what Vertical Line Finder does.

questions.question: TRAP: Comparing the 48m jib to the 31m jib at 31m radius - why does the shorter jib have HIGHER capacity (2,930 kg vs 1,970 kg)?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is correct engineering - shorter jibs are more efficient at their maximum radius.

options.text: The 31m jib weighs less, leaving more capacity for the load at that radius
options.explanation: Correct. At 31m radius, the 48m jib has 17m of extra jib extending beyond the load point, creating additional moment from the jib's own weight. The 31m jib is at its tip, with no excess jib weight to reduce capacity.
Incorrect. Same reeving configuration is used regardless of jib length.
Incorrect. Wind isn't the primary factor in these chart differences.

questions.question: How many tower sections can be used with the 91 K, and what is the maximum tower height?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The crane can accommodate more sections.
Correct. The chart shows configurations from 0 to 6 tower sections, with maximum tower height of 48m. Each section adds approximately 8m of height.
Incorrect. Self-erecting cranes don't reach these heights.
Incorrect. More sections are available.

questions.question: The counterweight table shows two configurations: 16 × 2,500 kg at r=2.75m and 14 × 2,500 kg at r=3.5m. Why use different configurations?

options.text: Different jib lengths require different counterweights
options.explanation: Incorrect. The counterweight relates to base stability, not jib length directly.

options.text: Placing counterweights further from center (3.5m) requires fewer blocks to achieve the same stabilizing moment
options.explanation: Correct. Moment = Weight × Distance. At 3.5m radius: 14 × 2,500 × 3.5 = 122,500 kNm. At 2.75m: 16 × 2,500 × 2.75 = 110,000 kNm. The further placement creates more moment per block, so fewer are needed.

options.text: The lighter configuration is for transport only
options.explanation: Incorrect. Both are operational configurations.

options.text: Wind conditions determine which to use
options.explanation: Incorrect. Site geometry typically determines counterweight placement.

questions.question: What is the operating weight of the 91 K crane?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the maximum lifting capacity, not crane weight.
Correct. The chart shows the crane's operating weight as approximately 20,600 kg (about 20.6 tonnes). This is important for site planning and ground bearing calculations.
Incorrect. This is the counterweight total, not crane weight.
Incorrect. This is the tip capacity.

questions.question: What is Speed2Lift mode?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Speed2Lift relates to hoisting, not slewing.

options.text: Pure 2-fall operation with maximum hoist speed for lighter loads
options.explanation: Correct. Speed2Lift uses 2-fall reeving to maximize hoisting speed when lifting lighter loads. Higher speed means faster cycle times but reduced capacity compared to 4-fall operation.
Incorrect. Speed2Lift is for normal operations, not emergencies.

options.text: Automatic speed adjustment based on wind
options.explanation: Incorrect. Speed2Lift is reeving-related, not wind-related.

questions.question: COMPLEX: A contractor needs to lift 2,500 kg to a radius of 26m. Which jib configuration provides sufficient capacity with the smallest jib?

options.explanation: Correct. The 31m jib shows 3,290 kg capacity at 26m radius in LM 1 mode - sufficient for 2,500 kg with margin. The 37m jib also works (2,680 kg) but is longer than necessary.
Incorrect. While this works, the 31m jib is smaller and provides adequate capacity.
Incorrect. 2,440 kg is less than the 2,500 kg required - this jib is insufficient.
Incorrect. Shorter jibs can handle this lift.

questions.question: What advantage does a self-erecting crane like the 91 K offer over traditional tower cranes?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Self-erecting cranes typically have lower capacity than large tower cranes.

options.text: Can erect itself without a mobile crane - faster setup and lower mobilization cost
options.explanation: Correct. Self-erecting cranes fold for road transport and erect themselves hydraulically on site. No expensive mobile crane is needed for assembly, making them cost-effective for smaller projects.
Incorrect. Traditional tower cranes often have longer reach.
Incorrect. Wind limits are similar or more restrictive for self-erecting cranes.

questions.question: The chart shows maximum hoisting speed of 76.1 m/min for 400 kg loads and 12.7 m/min for 6,000 kg loads. What does this demonstrate?

options.text: The crane has two different motors
options.explanation: Incorrect. It's the same 15 kW motor - power is distributed differently.

options.text: Constant power output - lighter loads can be hoisted faster (Power = Force × Speed)
options.explanation: Correct. With 15 kW fixed power, lifting 6,000 kg (high force) limits speed to 12.7 m/min. Lifting 400 kg allows 76.1 m/min. The motor's power output remains constant while the speed-load relationship varies inversely.

options.text: Safety regulations require slower speeds for heavy loads
options.explanation: Incorrect. The relationship is physics-based, not regulatory.

options.text: The rope can't handle fast speeds with heavy loads
options.explanation: Incorrect. Rope capacity isn't the limiting factor here.

charts.name: Potain Igo T 139

Incorrect. ISO 9001 is a quality management standard, not a crane design standard.
Incorrect. OSHA is US operational regulations, not crane design standards.

questions.question: With the 55m jib at 0° (horizontal) in standard mode (Configuration 1), what is the capacity at 55m radius (jib tip)?

Incorrect. Check the load chart for the correct tip capacity.

questions.question: What is the maximum lifting capacity of the Igo T 139 and at what radius?

Incorrect. 4,000 kg is the maximum capacity when the jib is raised to 30°.

questions.question: CRITICAL: The Igo T 139 has three jib angle options: 0°, 10°, and 30°. What happens to maximum capacity when the jib is raised from 0° to 30°?

options.text: Capacity increases because the jib is shorter
options.explanation: Incorrect. Capacity decreases, not increases.

options.text: Capacity decreases from 8,000 kg to 4,000 kg
options.explanation: Correct. At 0° (horizontal), max capacity is 8,000 kg. At 30°, max capacity drops to only 4,000 kg. The raised jib position changes the crane's geometry and reduces lifting capacity.

options.text: Capacity stays the same at 8,000 kg
options.explanation: Incorrect. The jib angle significantly affects capacity.
Incorrect. Raising the jib reduces, not increases capacity.

questions.question: What is Potain Plus mode?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Potain Plus is an operational mode, not a subscription.

options.text: An enhanced capacity mode that increases load limits above standard curves
options.explanation: Correct. Potain Plus provides higher capacities than standard mode. For example, at 55m tip capacity increases from 1,000 kg (standard) to 1,200 kg (Potain Plus) - a 20% increase.
Incorrect. Potain Plus affects capacity, not speed.
Incorrect. Anti-collision is a separate feature.

questions.question: Using the 55m jib at 0°, what is the capacity at 30m radius in standard mode vs Potain Plus mode?

options.explanation: Correct. At 30m with the 55m horizontal jib: standard mode shows 2,300 kg while Potain Plus shows 2,550 kg - an increase of 250 kg (about 11%).
Incorrect. You have the values reversed - Plus mode has higher capacity.
Incorrect. Potain Plus provides increased capacity over standard mode.
Incorrect. These values are too high for 30m radius with 55m jib.

questions.question: TRAP: When the jib is raised to 10°, what happens to the capacity across most of the working range?

options.text: Capacity remains high (8,000 kg) at close radii
options.explanation: Incorrect. At 10° jib angle, capacity is dramatically reduced.

options.text: Capacity drops to a constant 1,300 kg across most radii
options.explanation: Correct. At 10° jib angle, capacity is limited to only 1,300 kg across most of the working range. This is a significant reduction from 8,000 kg at 0°. The 10° position extends reach but severely limits capacity.

options.text: Capacity increases due to better geometry
options.explanation: Incorrect. The 10° position has much lower capacity than horizontal.
Incorrect. 4,000 kg is the max at 30°, not 10°.

questions.question: What is the total counterweight required for operating the Igo T 139 at 50m jib length?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is insufficient for full operation.
Correct. The chart specifies 48,600 kg of counterweight consisting of 12 ballast blocks at 4,050 kg each. This is required for stable operation at 50m jib configuration.
Incorrect. Check the counterweight specifications on the chart.
Incorrect. This exceeds the specified counterweight.

questions.question: How many different jib length configurations are available for the Igo T 139?

options.explanation: Incorrect. More jib lengths are available.
Correct. The load chart shows capacity tables for 5 different jib lengths: 33.4m, 42m, 47m, 50m, and 55m. Shorter jibs have higher tip capacity but less reach.
Incorrect. The available lengths are different.
Incorrect. Multiple jib configurations are available.

questions.question: COMPLEX: A contractor needs to lift 3,500 kg at 24m radius. With the jib horizontal (0°), which is the SHORTEST jib configuration that can make this lift?

options.explanation: Correct. The 33.4m jib shows 5,100 kg capacity at 24m in standard mode - more than sufficient for 3,500 kg. Shorter jibs have higher capacity at the same radius.
Incorrect. While the 42m jib works (4,500 kg at 24m), a shorter jib is available.
Incorrect. The 47m jib works but the 33.4m is shorter.
Incorrect. The 55m jib has the lowest capacity at 24m (3,000 kg standard) - still sufficient but not the shortest option.

questions.question: What tower height configurations are available with the 55m jib?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Multiple height configurations are available.

options.text: H+0, H+1, H+2, and H+3 (ranging from 19.7m to 40.7m under-hook height)
options.explanation: Correct. The chart shows four height configurations for the 55m jib: H+0 (19.7-22.7m), H+1 (25.7-28.7m), H+2 (31.7-34.7m), and H+3 (37.7-40.7m). Height depends on tower sections installed.
Incorrect. More configurations are available.
Incorrect. Maximum hook height is approximately 40.7m.

questions.question: What is the maximum road transport speed for the Igo T 139?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the maximum wind speed for erection, not transport speed.
Correct. The transport axle (J215M) is rated for 80 km/h road transport. The crane folds into a compact configuration for highway transport.
Incorrect. The crane can be transported faster than this.
Incorrect. This would exceed safe transport speeds for construction equipment.

questions.question: What is the Power Control function on the Igo T 139?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Power Control is about winch speed adaptation, not voltage.

options.text: Adapts winch speeds to the available power supply
options.explanation: Correct. Power Control automatically adjusts winch speeds based on the available electrical power. This allows the crane to operate efficiently on sites with limited power supply by reducing speeds rather than overloading the supply.
Incorrect. Emergency systems are separate from Power Control.
Incorrect. Power Control relates to grid power adaptation, not solar.

questions.question: TRAP: Why would an operator choose the 10° jib angle over the 0° horizontal position?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The 10° position has much LOWER capacity (1,300 kg vs 8,000 kg max).

options.text: To extend reach with light loads - the 10° position allows reaching further at constant 1,300 kg capacity
options.explanation: Correct. At 10°, the 55m jib can reach up to 54.2m radius (vs 55m at 0°) while maintaining 1,300 kg capacity. It's useful when you need extra height clearance or specific geometry for light loads, not for heavy lifts.

options.text: It's the only position allowed in high winds
options.explanation: Incorrect. Wind restrictions are separate from jib angle choice.
Incorrect. Counterweight is the same regardless of jib angle during operation.
