questions.question: What is the out-of-service wind speed rating specified in EN 14439-C25?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is lower than the actual rating.
Correct. The chart specifies EN 14439-C25 out-of-service proof with wind from each side at 100 km/h. This is the storm condition the crane must withstand when not operating.
Incorrect. This exceeds the C25 standard rating.
Incorrect. The chart clearly states the wind requirement.

questions.question: What power supply does the SK 415-20 require?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Tower cranes require three-phase power.
Correct. The crane requires 132 kVA three-phase power at either 480V/60Hz (US standard) or 400V/50Hz (European standard). This is significantly more power than smaller cranes.
Incorrect. Residential power is insufficient for tower cranes.
Incorrect. 66 kW is the hoist motor power, not total power requirement.

questions.question: COMPLEX: A load weighs 15,000 lb and needs to reach 180.4 ft radius. Using the 196.9 ft jib, which reeving configuration can make this lift?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Check the 2-fall capacity at 180.4 ft radius.

options.text: Only 4-fall (44,093 lb line) - 2-fall shows only 6,173 lb at 180.4 ft while 4-fall shows 16,094 lb
options.explanation: Correct. At 180.4 ft with 196.9 ft jib: 2-fall capacity is only 6,173 lb (insufficient). The 4-fall line shows 16,094 lb capacity (sufficient for 15,000 lb). You must use 4-fall reeving.
Incorrect. The 2-fall line has insufficient capacity at this radius.
Incorrect. The 4-fall configuration can make this lift.

questions.question: Converting the maximum jib length: 263 ft equals approximately how many meters?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Feet and meters are not equivalent - 1 foot ≈ 0.3 meters.
Correct. 1 meter = 3.28 feet. So 263 ft ÷ 3.28 = 80.2 m. This is important when comparing imperial and metric crane specifications.
Incorrect. This uses the wrong conversion factor.
Incorrect. This significantly overestimates the length.

questions.question: TRAP: Why does the shorter 114.8 ft jib configuration show capacity of 31,747 lb at its tip (111.5 ft), while the longer 262.5 ft jib only shows 5,071 lb at the same radius (using 2-fall)?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is correct engineering behavior.

options.text: The 114.8 ft jib uses 4-fall (MC) line at its tip, while the long jib table shows 2-fall capacity
options.explanation: Correct. The short jib configurations show 4-fall (44,093 lb line) capacity. At 111.5 ft: the short jib's 4-fall capacity is 31,747 lb. The 262.5 ft jib 2-fall capacity at similar radius (~114.8 ft) is 14,771 lb. Different reeving AND less jib weight both contribute to higher capacity.
Incorrect. The main factors are reeving configuration and jib weight.
Incorrect. Wind isn't the factor here.

charts.name: Terex CTL 260A-18
charts.description: Luffing jib tower crane with 60m max jib. 18 tonne max capacity. Features T-Link, Anticollision, and Zoning systems. Three reeving configurations (9t, 13.5t, 18t).

questions.question: What type of crane is the Terex CTL 260A-18?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Flat-top cranes use trolley travel along a horizontal jib.
Correct. The CTL 260A-18 is a luffing jib crane - the jib pivots up and down to change radius. The 'CTL' designation indicates Terex's luffing crane series. The hook is fixed at the jib tip.
Incorrect. This is a conventional tower crane requiring mobile crane assembly.
Incorrect. Hammerhead cranes have a horizontal jib with trolley.

questions.question: What is the maximum lifting capacity of the CTL 260A-18?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the medium reeving line capacity.
Incorrect. This is the intermediate reeving line capacity.
Correct. Maximum capacity is 18 tonnes using the heaviest reeving configuration (4-fall). This is achieved at radii up to approximately 17.9m with the 40m jib configuration.
Incorrect. This is the tip capacity at maximum 60m radius.

questions.question: The load chart shows three capacity lines: 9t, 13.5t, and 18t. What do these represent?

options.explanation: Incorrect. These are all for the same CTL 260A-18 crane.

options.text: Different reeving configurations (2-fall, 3-fall, 4-fall) with different max capacities
options.explanation: Correct. The 9t line uses lighter reeving for faster speeds at moderate loads. The 13.5t line is intermediate. The 18t line uses maximum reeving (4-fall) for heavy lifts but slower speeds. Each has different reach capabilities.
Incorrect. Wind ratings don't determine capacity lines.
Incorrect. While counterweight varies, the primary difference is reeving configuration.

questions.question: Using the 60m jib with the 9t line, what is the capacity at 50m radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the tip capacity at 60m.
Correct. At 50m radius with the 60m jib using the 9t line, capacity is 3.30 tonnes. The 9t line provides extended reach but lower capacity at longer radii.
Incorrect. This is the 13.5t/18t line capacity at 50m with shorter jibs.
Incorrect. 9t is only available at close radii (under ~34m).

questions.question: COMPLEX: You need to lift 10 tonnes to 35m radius using the 45m jib. Which reeving line can make this lift?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The 9t line's maximum capacity is only 9 tonnes.

options.text: 13.5t line - shows 10.04t capacity at 35m with 45m jib
options.explanation: Correct. At 35m radius with 45m jib: the 9t line maxes at 9t (insufficient). The 13.5t line shows 10.04t (sufficient). The 18t line shows 10.04t (also works but slower). Choose 13.5t for better speed.
Incorrect. Both 13.5t and 18t lines can make this lift.
Incorrect. The 13.5t and 18t lines can both handle this load.

questions.question: What is the jib angle range for the CTL 260A-18?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Luffing cranes typically have much steeper angles.
Correct. The jib luffs from 15° (nearly horizontal, maximum radius) to 85° (nearly vertical, minimum radius). At 85° the hook is close to the tower for minimum radius operations.
Incorrect. The jib is adjustable, not fixed.
Incorrect. The maximum angle is 85°, and it's variable.

questions.question: What is the minimum out-of-service jib angle?

options.explanation: Incorrect. 15° is the in-service minimum angle (max radius).
Correct. When out of service (parked), the jib must be raised to 55-65° to reduce the wind sail area and moment arm. This is higher than the in-service minimum of 15°.
Incorrect. 85° is maximum angle, not the out-of-service requirement.
Incorrect. Leaving a luffing jib horizontal out of service creates dangerous wind loads.

questions.question: The CTL 260A-18 can use three different tower systems. Which provides the maximum height?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is not the tallest option.

options.text: HD23 tower with R3 basement - up to 84.40m
options.explanation: Correct. The HD23 heavy-duty tower with R3 basement configuration provides maximum hook height of 84.40m (under FEM 1001 with 30m jib). This makes it suitable for high-rise construction.
Incorrect. TS23 is similar height to H20.

options.text: All towers have the same height
options.explanation: Incorrect. Tower heights vary significantly by system.

questions.question: What special features are listed for the CTL 260A-18?

options.explanation: Incorrect. These are Terex flat-top crane features.

Correct. The crane features T-Link (telematics/connectivity), Anticollision (prevents crane-to-crane or crane-to-obstacle collisions), and Zoning (restricts operation to defined areas). These are modern safety and management systems.
Incorrect. These are WOLFF crane features.
Incorrect. Several advanced features are listed.

questions.question: IMPORTANT: The load chart includes a note about rope weight. What adjustment may be needed?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The chart specifically notes rope weight is not included.

options.text: Deduct rope weight (1.3 kg/m) from capacity for actual lifting height
options.explanation: Correct. The chart states 'Load diagram not consider rope weight: p = 1.3 kg/m'. For tall lifts, you must deduct the weight of rope paid out. Example: 100m of rope = 130 kg deduction from capacity.
Incorrect. This would be unsafe - capacity should be reduced, not increased.
Incorrect. Rope weight affects all configurations.

questions.question: What are the two luffing winch options and their full-cycle times?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Two luffing winch options are available.

options.text: LFC 45 (45 kW, 2.5 min cycle) or LFC 67 (67 kW, 1.8 min cycle)
options.explanation: Correct. The LFC 45 provides 2.5 minutes for a full luff cycle (15° to 85°), while the more powerful LFC 67 completes the same cycle in 1.8 minutes. Faster luffing improves productivity on repetitive lift cycles.
Incorrect. Luffing cycles are longer than 1 minute.
Incorrect. Modern luffing cranes are much faster.

questions.question: What power supply does the CTL 260A-18 require with the LFC 67 luffing winch?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Tower cranes require three-phase power and more capacity.
Correct. With the LFC 67 luffing winch, the crane requires 165 kVA average consumption at 400V-50Hz (European) or 460V-60Hz (American). The LFC 45 option requires 123 kVA.
Incorrect. Residential power is insufficient.
Incorrect. 45 kW is just the luffing motor power, not total supply.

questions.question: TRAP: Looking at the 45m jib configuration, why does the chart show '*' with '+ 200 kg' for some entries?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is intentional notation.

options.text: Shorter jib configurations (30-50m) get +200 kg bonus capacity on the 13.5t line
options.explanation: Correct. The note '* = +200 kg [50m - 45m - 40m - 35m - 30m]' indicates that for shorter jib configurations using the 13.5t line, an additional 200 kg capacity is available. This accounts for reduced jib self-weight on shorter configurations.
Incorrect. The asterisk indicates an addition, not a deduction.
Incorrect. This misinterprets the notation entirely.

questions.question: The chart shows two standards: FEM 1005 C25 and FEM 1001. What is the difference?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Both are European standards.

options.text: FEM 1001 allows greater tower heights than FEM 1005 C25 for the same jib length
options.explanation: Correct. FEM 1001 is an older, more permissive standard. FEM 1005 with C25 classification is the current EN 14439 equivalent with stricter requirements. The height tables show FEM 1001 often permits taller configurations than FEM 1005 C25.
Incorrect. The standards have different height allowances.
Incorrect. It's the opposite - FEM 1001 is typically more permissive.

questions.question: COMPLEX: You're lifting 6 tonnes to 40m radius using the 55m jib. Using the 13.5t line, the chart shows 6.13t capacity. What is the actual available capacity considering 80m of rope out?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Rope weight must be considered for tall lifts.

options.text: 6.03 t (6.13t minus 0.104t rope weight: 80m × 1.3 kg/m = 104 kg)
options.explanation: Correct. Rope weight: 80m × 1.3 kg/m = 104 kg = 0.104t. Chart capacity 6.13t - 0.104t = 6.026t available. Since the load is 6.00t, this lift is still possible but with minimal margin. Always verify rope deduction on tall lifts!
Incorrect. Rope weight reduces available capacity, not increases it.
Incorrect. This underestimates the available capacity.

charts.name: Krøll K630F
charts.description: Danish flat-top tower crane with 24 tonne max capacity. Features stepless controls, 2-part and 4-part reeving options. FEM 1.001 and EN 14439 certified.

questions.question: What type of crane is the Krøll K630F?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The K630F uses a horizontal jib with trolley travel.

Correct. The K630F is a flat-top design with no cathead/peak above the jib. The trolley travels along the horizontal jib to change radius. Krøll is a Danish manufacturer known for heavy-duty flat-top cranes.
Incorrect. This is a conventional tower crane requiring mobile crane assembly.
Incorrect. This is a fixed tower crane.

questions.question: What is the maximum lifting capacity of the K630F?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the maximum for 2-part reeving only.
Correct. Maximum capacity is 24 tonnes using 4-part reeving. The crane can lift up to 12 tonnes with 2-part reeving for faster speeds on lighter loads.
Incorrect. This is well below the crane's maximum.
Incorrect. This exceeds the crane's rating.

questions.question: The K630F offers 2-part and 4-part reeving. What is the relationship between them?

options.text: 4-part has double the capacity but half the speed of 2-part
options.explanation: Correct. 4-part reeving doubles the mechanical advantage (24t vs 12t max), but the hoist speed is halved. Example: at light loads, 2-part reaches 136 m/min while 4-part reaches 68 m/min. Choose based on load weight and required cycle time.

options.text: Both have the same capacity and speed
options.explanation: Incorrect. The reeving configurations have different capacity and speed characteristics.
Incorrect. More parts of line = more capacity.
Incorrect. More parts of line = slower speed.

questions.question: What is the maximum hoist speed with 2-part reeving when lifting loads under 1.2 tonnes?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the speed at higher loads (5.0-12.0t).
Correct. With 2-part reeving and loads 0-1.2 tonnes, maximum hoist speed is 136 m/min. This is the fastest hoisting speed available on this crane, used for light loads or empty hook travel.
Incorrect. This is the light-load speed for 4-part reeving.
Incorrect. This is the slowest 4-part speed at maximum load.

questions.question: What is the hoist speed when lifting 20 tonnes using 4-part reeving?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This speed is for loads under 2.4 tonnes.
Incorrect. This speed is for loads 2.4-10.0 tonnes.
Correct. For loads 10.0-24.0 tonnes using 4-part reeving, hoist speed is 17 m/min. Heavy loads require slower speeds to maintain control and stay within motor power limits.
Incorrect. This is the maximum 2-part speed for very light loads.

questions.question: What is the hoist motor power on the K630F?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the trolley motor power.
Incorrect. This is the combined slew motor power.
Correct. The hoist winch motor is 85 kW - the largest motor on the crane. This powers the main lifting function with the 550m drum capacity.
Incorrect. This is the travel motor total.

questions.question: How much hoist rope can the drum hold?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the trolley drum capacity.
Correct. The hoist drum holds 550 meters of rope across 6 layers. This allows for very tall tower configurations. The 20mm diameter wire has a 330 kN minimum breaking load with safety factor 5.
Incorrect. The actual capacity is much larger.
Incorrect. Tower cranes need much more rope capacity.

questions.question: What is the slewing configuration on the K630F?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The slew uses multiple smaller motors.
Correct. The slew drive uses three 10 kW motors (30 kW total) providing variable speed from 0 to 0.6 rpm. This multi-motor arrangement provides redundancy and smooth control.
Incorrect. This is the travel (undercarriage) motor configuration.
Incorrect. 85 kW is the hoist motor.

questions.question: What is the trolley travel speed on the K630F?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the rail travel speed.
Correct. Trolley speed is 0-70 m/min regardless of load (0-24t). The 16 kW trolley motor provides this speed with stepless variable control for precise positioning.
Incorrect. This is the maximum hoist speed.
Incorrect. This is the slew speed (rotation), not linear trolley speed.

questions.question: What power supply does the K630F require?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Tower cranes require three-phase power.
Correct. The crane accepts both European (400V/50Hz) and American (480V/60Hz) power standards. It consumes 150 kVA with a 160A main fuse. A 350 kVA generator is recommended for site power.
Incorrect. Tower cranes use AC mains power.
Incorrect. 85 kW is the hoist motor, not total supply.

questions.question: What generator size does Krøll recommend for the K630F?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Generators need headroom above consumption.

Correct. Krøll recommends a 350 kVA generator - more than double the 150 kVA average consumption. This accounts for motor starting currents, simultaneous operations, and power factor. Never size a generator exactly to consumption.
Incorrect. This is the hoist motor power only.
Incorrect. This is far too small.

questions.question: What supply cable is required for a 200m cable run to the crane?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is only suitable up to 175m.
Correct. For cable lengths 175-250m, use 4 × 95 mm². For 0-175m, 4 × 70 mm² is sufficient. Longer runs need larger cables to compensate for voltage drop.
Incorrect. This is undersized for 200m.
Incorrect. Three-phase power requires 4 conductors.

questions.question: What standards is the K630F certified to?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is an American standard; Krøll is European.

Correct. The crane meets FEM 1.001 (European crane federation standard, HC1 duty class, A3/E3 classifications) and EN 14439:2009 with C25 wind rating. These are the key European tower crane standards.
Incorrect. European cranes must be certified.
Incorrect. ISO 9001 is quality management, not crane design.

questions.question: What special control feature does the K630F offer?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The crane has more sophisticated controls.

options.text: Completely stepless controls with creeping speed down to 0.5% of maximum
options.explanation: Correct. All motions (hoist, trolley, slew, travel) are stepless - meaning infinitely variable, not stepped speeds. Creeping speed can go as low as 0.5% of maximum for precise positioning. This ensures smooth, jerk-free operation.
Incorrect. The crane uses stepless (variable) control.
Incorrect. The crane has sophisticated electronic controls.

questions.question: COMPLEX: You need to lift 8 tonnes. Which reeving should you use and what will be the approximate hoist speed?

options.explanation: Incorrect. 2-part maximum capacity is 12t, but 8t falls in the 5.0-12.0t range at only 34 m/min.

options.text: 2-part at 34 m/min - within 12t limit, fastest option for this load
options.explanation: Correct. 8 tonnes is within 2-part capacity (12t max). At 5.0-12.0t range, speed is 34 m/min. With 4-part, 8t would be in the 2.4-10.0t range at 37 m/min - slightly faster but unnecessary complexity. 2-part is the practical choice.
Incorrect. 8 tonnes doesn't require 4-part reeving.
Incorrect. Both reeving options can handle 8 tonnes.

charts.name: Pecco SK 180
charts.description: Flat-top tower crane with 196 ft (60m) max jib. 27,600 lbs (12.5t) max capacity with 4-part line. Distributed by Morrow Equipment. Features 4-speed gearbox and top climbing capability.

questions.question: What type of crane is the Pecco SK 180?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The SK 180 uses a horizontal jib with trolley travel.
Correct. The Pecco SK 180 is a flat-top design with horizontal jib and trolley. It's distributed in North America by Morrow Equipment. The trolley travels along the jib to change radius.
Incorrect. This is a conventional tower crane requiring mobile crane assembly.
Incorrect. This is a fixed tower crane.

questions.question: What is the maximum lifting capacity of the SK 180?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the 2-part line maximum capacity.
Correct. Maximum capacity is 27,600 lbs (12,500 kg) using 4-part reeving. The 2-part line maxes at 13,800 lbs (6,250 kg) but offers faster hoisting speeds for lighter loads.
Incorrect. This is the tip capacity at maximum radius.
Incorrect. This exceeds the crane's maximum rating.

questions.question: Using the 196 ft jib with 2-part line, what is the capacity at 150 ft radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Full capacity is only available at closer radii.
Correct. At 150 ft radius with the 196 ft jib using 2-part line, capacity is 6,700 lbs (3,040 kg). Capacity decreases as radius increases.
Incorrect. This is the tip capacity at 196 ft.
Incorrect. This is the capacity at 101 ft radius.

questions.question: Using the 196 ft jib with 4-part line, what is the capacity at 120 ft radius?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Maximum 4-part capacity requires much closer radius.

Correct. At 120 ft radius with 196 ft jib using 4-part line, capacity is 8,100 lbs (3,670 kg). Note that 4-part doesn't always give higher capacity at long radii - compare with 2-part which gives 8,700 lbs at same radius.
Incorrect. This is the 4-part tip capacity.
Incorrect. Check the 4-part load chart at 120 ft.

questions.question: TRAP: At 120 ft radius with the 196 ft jib, which reeving configuration gives HIGHER capacity?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is a common misconception. At longer radii, 2-part can actually have higher capacity.

options.text: 2-part: 8,700 lbs vs 4-part: 8,100 lbs - the 2-part line wins!
options.explanation: Correct. Surprisingly, at 120 ft radius the 2-part line (8,700 lbs) beats the 4-part line (8,100 lbs). This is because the 4-part line starts at higher capacity (27,600 vs 13,800 lbs) but the curves cross at longer radii. Always check both charts!
Incorrect. They have different capacities at this radius.
Incorrect. Both configurations can operate at 120 ft radius.

questions.question: What are the five available jib lengths on the SK 180?

options.explanation: Incorrect. These are not the actual jib options.

Correct. The SK 180 offers five jib configurations. Shorter jibs have higher tip capacity - the 101 ft jib can lift 13,800 lbs (2-part) at its tip, while the 196 ft jib only lifts 4,900 lbs at its tip.
Incorrect. Multiple jib lengths are available.
Incorrect. These aren't the correct jib lengths.

questions.question: What is the hoist motor power and type?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The crane uses a larger AC motor.

options.text: 105 hp (78 kW) AC motor with 4-speed gearbox and eddy current brake
options.explanation: Correct. The hoist uses a 105 hp (78 kW) AC motor with a 4-speed gearbox featuring remote-controlled gear shifting. The eddy current brake provides smooth, controlled stopping.
Incorrect. 6.4 hp is the trolley motor power.
Incorrect. 350 kW is the minimum generator size, not motor power.

questions.question: What is the maximum hoist speed with 2-part line in Gear 4?

options.explanation: Incorrect. This is Gear 1 speed.

options.text: 440 fpm (134 m/min) for loads up to 5,500 lbs
options.explanation: Correct. Gear 4 with 2-part line reaches 440 fpm (134 m/min) - extremely fast for a tower crane! This speed is only available for loads up to 5,500 lbs (2,500 kg). Heavier loads require lower gears.
Incorrect. This is Gear 1 speed for 4-part line.
Incorrect. This is the trolley speed.

questions.question: What power supply does the SK 180 require?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Tower cranes require three-phase power.

options.text: 3-phase 480V, 60 Hz, 250 Ampere service, minimum 350 kW generator
options.explanation: Correct. The crane requires 3-phase 480V at 60 Hz (US standard) with 250A service. A minimum 350 kW generator is specified as critical for safe operation. Note: Do NOT use open Delta transformers.
Incorrect. This is a US-market specification using 480V/60Hz.
Incorrect. Specific power requirements must be met.

questions.question: The SK 180 uses two tower section types. What are they?

options.explanation: Incorrect. They're designated S16 and S35.

Correct. S16 sections are 14'-11" (4.55m) tall weighing 5,500 lbs. S35 sections are 19'-5" (5.92m) tall weighing 8,900 lbs. Configuration I uses S35 lower sections with S16 upper sections via a transition piece.
Incorrect. Two section types are used.
Incorrect. The designations are S16 and S35.

questions.question: What is the maximum freestanding height with S16 tower?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The crane can stand taller freestanding.

options.text: 128 ft (39.0m) standard, or 137'-11" (42.0m) with hydraulic top climbing unit
options.explanation: Correct. Maximum freestanding height is 128 ft with standard configuration, or nearly 138 ft with the hydraulic top climbing unit. Beyond this, tie-ins to the structure are required.
Incorrect. This is maximum tied height, not freestanding.
Incorrect. The crane can stand much taller.

questions.question: IMPORTANT: What special requirement applies when hook height exceeds 330 ft (100m)?

options.explanation: Incorrect. There's a critical safety requirement.

options.text: Reduce tower sections above uppermost tie-in by 1 section
options.explanation: Correct. When hook height exceeds 330 ft (100m), you MUST reduce the number of tower sections above the uppermost tie-in location by 1 section. This accounts for increased wind loads and moments at extreme heights.
Incorrect. The requirement relates to tower sections.
Incorrect. The requirement is about tower configuration.

questions.question: What is the swing (slewing) configuration?

options.explanation: Incorrect. The swing uses dual motors.
Correct. The swing drive uses two 6.4 hp (4.8 kW) motors providing 1 rpm rotation speed. Dual motors provide redundancy and smooth slewing control.
Incorrect. 105 hp is the hoist motor.
Incorrect. The crane uses electric motors.

questions.question: What are the tie-in spacing limits?

options.explanation: Incorrect. Tie-in spacing varies.
Correct. Tie-in spacing ranges from 44'-3" (13.5m) minimum to 103'-5" (31.5m) maximum. Actual spacing depends on site conditions, tower configuration, and structural analysis.
Incorrect. Tie-in spacing has strict limits.
Incorrect. Tie-in spacing is engineered, not per floor.

questions.question: COMPLEX: You need to lift 15,000 lbs to 90 ft radius using the 168 ft jib. Which reeving and what's the capacity?

options.explanation: Correct analysis that 2-part is insufficient, but there's a better answer.

options.text: 4-part required - shows 16,100 lbs capacity at 90 ft with 168 ft jib
options.explanation: Correct. The 2-part line maxes at 13,800 lbs (insufficient for 15,000 lbs). The 4-part line with 168 ft jib shows 16,100 lbs at 90 ft radius - sufficient with 1,100 lbs margin. Always verify the specific jib/radius combination.
Incorrect. 2-part cannot lift 15,000 lbs regardless of radius.
Incorrect. The 4-part configuration can handle this lift.
